coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent sensor based on CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs complexes for visual identification of goji berries origin
coredata.dc:creator: Suo, Yixin
Long, Wanjun
Qi, Linlin
Yao, Fangmi
Lei, Guanghua
Guan, Yuting
Hu, Zikang
Marchioni, Eric
Zhao, Minjie
Zhou, Li
Fu, Haiyan
coredata.dc:description: The price and quality of goji berries (GB) are closely linked to their origins. Therefore, identifying the geographical origins of GB produced in specific origin is important. In this work, a fluorescent sensor based on a glutathione modified quantum dots and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots complexes (CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs) with an internal filtration effect (IFE) was constructed to visual identify the origin of GB. The phenolic acid compounds in GB could quench the fluorescence of QDs and CDs through the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and the aggregation-induced quenching (ACQ), respectively. Due to the different contents of phenolic acid in GB from different origins, the fluorescent sensor based on CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs complexes could produce distinct and different fluorescent colors, from orange to blue under the dual reaction. Finally, partial least squares discriminant analysis (PLS-DA) model was applied to establish classification models for identifying the geographical origin of GB based on the fluorescent colors. The results showed that the accuracy of GB visual recognition could be increased from 68.9% to 93.3%. This proven that the proposed strategy has excellent potentials for identification of the geographical origins of GB.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Goji berry
Geographical origin
Nano-effect
Fluorescent visualization sensor
Chemometrics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Response mechanism of CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs to GB
Sensing strategy of CdTe-QDs and goji berries
Sensing strategy of N-CDs and goji berries
Sensing performance of CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs to GB from different origin
GB origin identification tracing based on fluorescence spectroscopic and visual RGB data
Precise traceability of GB based on fluorescence spectroscopic data
Accurate GB traceability based on visual RGB data

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 32122068, 22208381) and Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province (2022CFB458), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, South-Central Minzu University (CZZ23012; CZQ23044). The authors thank the Analytical & Measuring Center, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-Central Minzu University for the spectral analyses.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence emission spectra of CdTe-QDs (a), N-CDs (b), and CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs (c) before and after adding GB; Fluorescence emission spectra of goji berries from 5 major origin (d); (e) The UV–visible absorption spectra of CdTe-QDs and the fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs (f) fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs, CdTe-QDs and CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs.
TEM images of QDs (a), CDs (b) and CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs (c) before (left) and after (right) joining GB extracts.

The fluorescence color response of CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs after reacting with GB from different origins (inside the blue wire frame); Fluorescence color response of CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs to different organic acids (in black frame).
Schematic diagram of LUMO and HOMO of GSH ligand and different organic acids (a). The PET effect existing between GSH and Caffeic acid (b).
Fluorescence spectra before/after the reaction of GB with CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs (Left / Right).
Visualized results of partial batches of GB from 5 origins before (a) and after (b) reacting with CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs.
Fluorescence sensor for identification mechanism of GB origin.
Results of origin identification of GB based on PLS-DA spontaneous fluorescence, fluorescence quenching spectroscopy of composites, and fluorescence RGB values.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent sensor based on CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs complexes for visual identification of goji berries origin

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Equipe de Chimie Analytique des Molécules Bioactives et Pharmacognosie, Institut Pluridisciplinaire Hubert Curien (UMR 7178, CNRS/UDS)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Equipe de Chimie Analytique des Molécules Bioactives et Pharmacognosie, Institut Pluridisciplinaire Hubert Curien (UMR 7178, CNRS/UDS), 74 route du Rhin, 67400, Illkirch, France

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Yixin Suo and Wanjun Long equally contributed to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The price and quality of goji berries (GB) are closely linked to their origins. Therefore, identifying the geographical origins of GB produced in specific origin is important. In this work, a fluorescent sensor based on a glutathione modified quantum dots and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots complexes (CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs) with an internal filtration effect (IFE) was constructed to visual identify the origin of GB. The phenolic acid compounds in GB could quench the fluorescence of QDs and CDs through the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and the aggregation-induced quenching (ACQ), respectively. Due to the different contents of phenolic acid in GB from different origins, the fluorescent sensor based on CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs complexes could produce distinct and different fluorescent colors, from orange to blue under the dual reaction. Finally, partial least squares discriminant analysis (PLS-DA) model was applied to establish classification models for identifying the geographical origin of GB based on the fluorescent colors. The results showed that the accuracy of GB visual recognition could be increased from 68.9% to 93.3%. This proven that the proposed strategy has excellent potentials for identification of the geographical origins of GB.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Goji berry (Lycium barbarum L.) is usually considered a superfruit, having both medicinal and edible properties. Goji berries (GB) contain various bioactive and pharmacological components, including immune regulation [1], anti-aging [2], hypoglycemic, lipid-lowering [3], antitumor, and anti-mutagenic effect [4]. GB was widely planted in Asia, with China being the leading supplier of GB [5], with an annual production of 82,000 ha, accounting for more than 95.0% of the world's output [6]. Environment affects GB's metabolite accumulation, impacting product quality [7]. The main production areas of GB in China include Xinjiang, Inner Mongolia, Qinghai, Gansu, Ningxia, and other regions [8]. Among, Zhongning County, Ningxia, is the authentic origin of GB [7]. The price of Zhongning goji berries is often double or even higher than those from other regions. However, the market often substitutes goji berries from different origins as Zhongning goji berries for sale [9]. Therefore, the evidence that it is feasible to differentiate the geographical origins holds crucial significance for GB quality control and market supervision [10].

To protect the interests and health of consumers, researchers have developed a series of methods for identifying the origin of GB and have achieved good results, such as HPLC-MS [11], GC-MS [12], ICP-MS [13] and HSI [14]. However, large instrument methods generally had complex pretreatment, were time-consuming, and had high technical requirements for operators, making it difficult to achieve rapid identification of on-site GB's origins [7]. Therefore, it is crucial to explore new methods to quickly, efficiently, and accurately identify the origin of GB.

Fluorescence sensing methods present an attractive option, as they leverage specific interactions and spectral changes to offer rapid [15], efficient [16], and reliable visual identification of food origin [17], pesticide residue, and heavy metals, which was widely applied in various fields. Nanomaterials play a significant role in fluorescence sensing methods [18], due to their unique physical and chemical properties [19]. In fluorescence array sensors, the most commonly used nanomaterials were quantum dots (QDs) and carbon dots (CDs) [20]. QDs were semiconductor materials with remarkable fluorescence characteristics, such as high photostability, broad and continuous excitation spectra, narrow and symmetric emission spectra, long fluorescence lifetime, and tunability [21]. Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CDs), consisting of a carbon core and peripheral electron layers, displayed enhanced optical properties and energy absorption following doping, resulting in stable fluorescence signals [22]. These unique properties made QDs and CDs suitable for constructing fluorescence sensors, which were widely used in monitoring food safety [23].

However, most single fluorescence sensors face challenges like low photostability and poor visual discrimination, hindering their further use. Dual-emission composite fluorescence sensing systems, an improvement over single-signal methods, offered more accurate results and strong anti-interference capabilities. It calculated the ratio of fluorescence intensities from fluorophores to address interference factors, enhancing sensitivity and reliability. Furthermore, based on the various chemical reactions between dual-emission composite fluorescence materials and different substances, signal differences could be amplified, providing a more diverse colour response. In recent years, composite fluorescence nanomaterials have been successfully applied in the authenticity identification of food. For example, Wang et al. constructed an Al @ AuNCs fluorescence sensor based on various chemical reactions between Al3+, AuNCs and flavonoid compounds, enabling rapid visual identification of different regions of chrysanthemum [24]. Qing et al. successfully applied a composite fluorescence sensor array based on PDI derivatives to authenticate red wine and identified eight different types and origins of red wine [25]. However, no double-emission composite fluorescent sensors can be applied to trace the origin of GB.

This study aims to construct fluorescent sensor based on CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs complexes for visual identification the origins of GB. Firstly, glutathione-modified cadmium telluride quantum dots (CdTe-QDs, orange) and nitrogen-doped carbon (N-CDs, blue) were synthesized using the hydrothermal method, and CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs fluorescence sensors were constructed based on these two fluorescent materials. When reacted with GB, the phenolic acid compounds in GB could selectively quench the fluorescence of CdTe-QDs and N-CDs through PET and ACQ, respectively. Due to the varying content of phenolic acids in GB from different sources, the fluorescence colour of CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs changed from orange to blue. Then, the corresponding fluorescence spectra data were obtained, and the RGB values of the fluorescence color were extracted for data analysis using the PLS-DA algorithm. The PLS-DA model validated the visual sensing method's effectiveness. This is the first study of visual sensing for GB origin.

Reduced glutathione (C10H17N3O6S, GSH), cadmium chloride (CdCl2), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), sodium tellurite (Na2TeO3), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), urea (CH4N2O) and trisodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd; Quercetin, succinic acid, caffeic acid, chlorogenic acid, isochlorogenic acid B, gallic acid, ferulic acid, protocatechuic acid, vanillic acid, syringic acid, succinic acid, 3-hydroxycinnamic acid, 3-hydroxypropionic acid, 3- (4-hydroxyphenyl) propionic acid and nicotinic acid were purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biological Co., Ltd; All chemical reagents were analytical pure; Deionized water was prepared using the Molgeneral 220a water purification system; The cadmium telluride quantum dots (CdTe-QDs) and citric acid/urea carbon dots (N-CDs) used in the experiment were synthesized by our laboratory.

The fluorescence spectrum data were obtained from Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan); The fluorescence lifetime data were obtained from the Edinburgh FLS 1000 fluorescence spectrophotometer; The UV visible absorption spectrum data were obtained from the Mapada UV-1800PC UV visible spectrophotometer; The infrared spectral data were obtained by Thermo Fisher Nicolet 6700 Fourier transform mid-infrared spectrometer; The Zeta potential results were obtained from Zetasizer Nano ZS 90; All pH measurements were completed by the Mettler Toledo FE20Plus pH meter. The TEM characterization experiment was conducted by Talors F200X transmission electron microscopy (FEI, Chicago, USA). The TEM images were provided by Nano Measure 1.2 software. The visual image was captured by the Canon digital SLR camera EOS Kiss X6i. The RGB values of the photos were extracted using Photoshop software.

In 2022, 50 batches of GB samples were purchased from the Chinese herbal medicine markets in various regions, with 10 batches from each origin. These included 10 batches of Zhongning goji berries (ZNG), 10 batches of Xinjiang goji berries (XJG), 10 batches of Qinghai goji berries (QHG), 10 batches of Gansu goji berries (GSG) and 10 batches of Inner Mongolia goji berries (NMG) from Ningxia, Xinjiang, Qinghai, Gansu, Inner Mongolia and other northwest regions. The detailed information on the GB samples is shown in the supporting information in Table S1 .

All batches of samples were crushed into powder and passed through a 20-mesh sieve. Each batch of powder samples was collected three times to obtain more uniform and representative sample information. Next, each powder sample (0.05 g ± 1%) was weighed accurately and extracted with 5.0 mL ultrapure water use ultrasonically for 30 min at 100 Hz with heat preservation at 35 ℃. Then, the centrifuged supernatant (4000 rpm, 10 min) was filtered with a 0.22 µm filter membrane, which yielded a GB extract. The resulting solution was sealed and refrigerated at 4 ℃ for further use.
CdTe-QDs and N-CDs were synthesized hydrothermal and further incubated for CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs. The preparation process was schematically described in Fig. S1.

Synthesis of N-CDs blue luminescent CDs were prepared using a hydrothermal heating process. Briefly, citric acid (0.576 g) and urea (1.0809 g) were added to 15.0 mL of DDW. After being mixed thoroughly, the mixture was added to 200 ℃ for carbonization in the hydrothermal autoclave reactor for 10 h. After natural cooling, a brown solution (N-CDs) was obtained. Then, the brown mixture was filtered, and the insoluble material was thus removed. In addition, N-CDs was further purified using a dialysis bag (MWCO: 1000 Da). Finally, the homogenized solution was collected and stored at 4 ℃.

Synthesis of CdTe-QDs CdTe-QDs (Cd: Te=1:0.2) were synthesized using the previously described method [26] with some modifications. The details were as follows: first, CdCl2 (0.2284 g) and GSH (0.6146 g) were added to 40.0 mL of DDW and evenly stirred until completely dissolved. Next, the pH of the solution was adjusted to 10 by NaOH (1.0 mol / L). Afterwards, Na2TeO3 (0.0443 g) was added to the reaction solution, and the reaction was stirred for 5 min. After that, 0.0227 g NaBH4 was dissolved in 1 mL of DDW and transferred to the hydrothermal autoclave reactor. The reaction was heated in a 180 ℃ oven for 35 min. After the reaction and natural cooling, a CdTe-QDs solution with orange fluorescence was obtained. CdTe-QDs were centrifuged for 10 min at 4000 rpm, and the supernatant was filtered with a 0.22 µm microwell filter to remove large particles. Finally, the homogenized solution was collected and stored at 4 ℃ for later use.

Preparation of CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs sensor 4 mL CdTe-QDs and 3.0 mL N-CDs were added into 42.0 mL Tris-HCl buffer solution with pH 7.0 at 4:3 ratio and incubated at room temperature for 2 h to obtain CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs nano fluorescent sensor. Finally, the homogeneous solution was collected and stored at 4 ℃.

The voltage, acquisition spacing, and slit width of the Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer were set to 500 V, 0.2 nm, and 10 nm, respectively. In the two-dimensional fluorescence spectra of GB standards, the excitation wavelength (Ex) and emission wavelength range (Em) were set to 345 nm and 300–750 nm, respectively, to investigate the spectral differences before and after the reaction. GB fluorescence response testing method: the fluorescence spectra of 50 batches of GB samples before and after reaction with CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs (n = 3) were collected. The steps were as follows: first, 50.0 μL, 50.0 μL and 300.0 μL of GB extract (10.0 mg/mL) or standards (100.0 mg/mL), CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs and water were added to the cuvette at a ratio of 1:1:6. Measure the emission spectrum of the solution after 5 min and measure 3 times for each sample. For the convenience of practical application, all fluorescence spectra data were recorded under room greenhouse pressure.

CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs was added to a black 96 well plate to construct a single channel visual array sensor. Then, GB extracts from different origins were added to the array sensor. After 5 min of reaction, the EOS Kiss X6i camera was used to capture the fluorescence colour changes of CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs in the UV box (365.0 nm; all parameters remain unchanged from the factory settings). Repeat the shot 3 times under the same environmental conditions, so each sample had 3 independent RGB value arrays.

The obtained array sensor images were preprocessed using Photoshop and MATLAB R2016a and combined with PLS-DA to identify the main origin of goji berries. All calculation programs for preprocessing and chemometrics methods were written by our laboratory.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response mechanism of CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs to GB
ce:section.ce:para: CdTe-QDs and N-CDs were combined through the internal filtration effect (IFE), forming a composite fluorescence sensor with dual emissions at 425.0 nm and 615.0 nm. The CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs achieved visual and specific identification of goji berries from different origins through the PET effect of CdTe-QDs and the ACQ effect of N-CDs. On one hand, caffeic acid, ferulic acid and chlorogenic acid in GB can compete for electrons on QDs through the PET effect, thereby reducing the fluorescence of QDs. On the other hand, phenolic acids could effectively quench the fluorescence of CDs through the ACQ effect. Under the dual reaction with QDs and CDs, GB quenched the fluorescence of CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs. Due to the differences in the bioaccumulation process of GB from different regions, different fluorescence colours could be produced after the reaction of GB with CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs, resulting in a rapid visual differentiation effect from orange to blue. Scheme 1.

The mechanism of fluorescence amplification between CdTe-QDs and N-CDs was investigated. As shown in Fig. S2c, the TEM images of CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs displayed regular spherical shapes, with one larger and one smaller, and were uniformly dispersed in the solution. Their particle sizes were approximately 3.75 nm and 7.0 nm, respectively, with a distance between the two less than 10.0 nm. A significant spectral overlap between the UV–visible absorption spectrum of CdTe-QDs and the fluorescence emission spectrum of N-CDs was observed in Fig. 1. The fluorescence lifetime results of CdTe-QDs with N-CDs (Fig. S3) revealed that the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs was partially quenched by the added CdTe-QDs (F1/F0). However, the corresponding fluorescence lifetime remained unchanged (F1/F0), indicating that in the interaction between CdTe-QDs and N-CDs, the static quenching effect was predominant. This indicated that the interaction between CdTe-QDs and N-CDs might occur through the internal filtration effect (IFE) [27], which led to the loss of spectral properties of N-CDs and the reduction of fluorescence intensity [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing strategy of CdTe-QDs and goji berries
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM images of CdTe-QDs are shown in Fig. S2a. The CdTe-QDs were spherical with regular shape and uniformly dispersed in the solution, with particle size around 3.75 nm. We first investigated the fluorescence quenching between CdTe-QDs and GB using Ningxia samples. The TEM results before and after adding GB exhibited that CdTe-QDs were evenly distributed in Fig. 2 a, and CdTe-QDs clustered significantly after adding GB. FT-IR analysis revealed peaks at 1730, 1260, 1410, 1050, and 3330 cm−1 [29], indicating the presence of polyhydroxy compounds in GB [30] (Fig. S4b black line). In addition, XPS results (Fig. S5) indicated enrichment of S, C, N, Cd, O, and Te elements in CdTe-QDs. XPS analysis confirmed the presence of GSH as a stabilizer on the surface of prepared CdTe-QDs. In the high-resolution S1s spectrum of CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs, a characteristic diffraction peak of the thiol group (SH) of GSH was observed at 163 eV. Therefore, after adding GB, CdTe-QDs reacts with polyhydroxyl compounds in GB to form carboxylate complexes, which are accompanied by the aggregation of CdTe-QDs. Fig. 1 a showed that the fluorescence intensity at 615.0 nm decreased significantly after adding GB. After adding GB, in the high-resolution C1s spectrum, the area of the C-O peak at 286.0 eV decreased, while the areas of the CC and COO- peaks at 287.7 eV and 288.7 eV increased. Additionally, in the high-resolution O1s spectrum, the area of the O1s peak at 531.2 eV decreased, while the area of the CO peak at 532.4 eV increased, indicating that the hydroxyl group decreased and the carboxylate complexes increased. This further confirmed the formation of carboxylate complexes after the addition of GB.

To verify the fluorescence fingerprint response properties of CdTe-QDs to GB, the response properties of representative phenolic compounds in GB to CdTe-QDs were investigated. As shown in Fig. 3, the compounds reflected different degrees of fluorescence quenching of CdTe-QDs, resulting in various colour changes. The results demonstrated that CdTe-QDs exhibited good fluorescence response to p-hydroxyphenyl propanic acid, caffeic acid, ferulic acid, succinic acid and chlorogenic acid. According to the XPS results (Fig. S5), it was found that the C1s peak between the individual compound succinic acid and CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs was consistent with the C1s peak between GB and CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs.

Furthermore, the density functional theory (DFT) method was applied to calculate the HOMO and LUMO energy levels of the quantum dot surface ligands GSH and five representative phenolic acids. The results were presented in Fig. 4 b. GSH had a higher LUMO (−0.01221 eV) energy level than Caffeic acid (−0.06309 eV), indicating potential PET due to overlapping orbital energies [31]. It illustrated that the excited electrons of GSH can be transferred from LUMO of GSH to LUMO of caffeic acid, thus losing the fluorescence and quenching the fluorescence of the QDs. Based on the above results, we speculated that large amounts of phenolic acids in GB were able to form complexes with CdTe-QDs. Moreover, since the LUMO level of quantum dot surface ligands (−0.01221 eV) was higher than the LUMO level of most phenolic acids, there may be a PET effect between CdTe-QDs + GB, leading to electron transfer and fluorescence quenching on CdTe-QDs. Furthermore, the UV–visible absorption spectrum of CdTe-QDs overlapped significantly with the fluorescence emission spectrum of GB (Fig. 1 e). This satisfies the conditions for the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) implementation [32]. This further confirms that phenolic acid compounds in the GB can compete for the electrons on the QDs through the PET effect, thus reducing the fluorescence of the QDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing strategy of N-CDs and goji berries
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM images of N-CDs were shown in Fig. S2b. The N-CDs were spherical with regular shape and uniformly dispersed in the solution, with particle size around 7.0 nm. Based on the FT-IR spectra results, we could observe that the N-CDs surface was rich in amino functional groups (Fig. S4a), which provided abundant reaction sites for GB. The TEM results before and after adding GB illustrated that N-CDs were evenly distributed. After the addition of GB, N-CDs accumulated significantly, and the particle size increased from 7.0 nm to about 20.0 nm, and their morphology was no longer a regular ellipsoid (Fig. 2 b). The results indicated that after the addition of GB, the amines in N-CDs reacted with phenolic acids containing polyhydroxyl in GB to form complexes and induce the aggregation of polyhydroxyl compounds. Additionally, Fig. 1 b presented that the fluorescence intensity at 425.0 nm decreased after adding GB. These observations indicated that ACQ occurred after adding GB.

In addition, fluorescence spectra analysis revealed that the emission spectra of five phenolic acid compounds overlapped with N-CDs, exhibiting strong fluorescence at 425.0 nm. After the reaction, the fluorescence intensity at 425.0 nm remained virtually unchanged, except for caffeic acid, which significantly quenched N-CDs fluorescence. This suggested that the interaction intensities between the compounds and N-CDs differed, resulting in various fluorescence colours. To further confirm the mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime spectra of N-CDs before and after adding GB were analyzed, and the fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs remained unchanged after adding GB, indicating static quenching dominance (Fig. S3e). The N-CDs aggregation or accumulations occur with the increase of GB concentration (Fig. S6). As a result, the space between molecules got narrower, which in turn caused the interaction force between N-CDs to get more robust [33]. Eventually, the possible aggregation-induced quenching effect (ACQ) between N-CDs and GB was further illustrated [34].

In conclusion, the fluorescence colour differences were finally amplified to accurately identify GB from different origins because phenolic acids in GB could produce different degrees of quenching or fluorescence enhancement with CdTe-QDs and N-CDs, respectively.

Furthermore, the discriminant mechanism of CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs was analyzed. As shown in Fig. 2 c, by observing the TEM results before and after adding GB, it was found that the aggregation of CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs was significantly increased after adding GB. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity at 425.0 nm and 615.0 nm reduced considerably (Fig. 1 c), which could result from the combination of CdTe-QDs and N-CDs with PET and ACQ effects. Moreover, we analyzed the FT-IR spectra of CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs before and after adding GB (Fig. S4b). It was found that after adding GB, the broad intensity peak at 3330 cm−1 disappeared, and the characteristic absorption band of -COOH weakened [35]. Additionally, the absorption peak of CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs at 1660 cm−1, which belonged to the CO stretch vibration peak of primary amine, redshifted to 1600 cm−1 [36,37], and the amide absorption peak at 3200 – 3400 cm−1 enhanced [38,39]. It was speculated that the phenolic acids in GB were bonded to the amine groups on the surface of CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs through hydrogen bonding. In addition, the zeta potential of the CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs was also changed in the presence of GB (Fig. S7) [40]. Because the particle size and surface groups of CdTe-QDs and N-CDs were inconsistent, the zeta potential of the composite (CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs) appeared double peaks. When GB was present, the zeta potential of the CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs was changed from bimodal to unimodal. The results showed that CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs aggregates, and more stable complexes with large particle size are obtained, thus the particle size and surface charge tended to be uniform [41]. The above results were also consistent with TEM characterization results. In conclusion, the composite fluorescent materials not only exhibited a rich color response but also possessed the ability to amplify fluorescence spectral differences through specific reactions with phenolic acids, resulting in fluorescent visualization results with different nano-effects and colors.

Therefore, the fluorescence sensing strategy established in this study could realize the identification of GB origin. Interestingly, under GB from different origins, fluorescence sensing showed different colours due to the differences in components in the GB from each origin. A colorimetric method was used to quickly identify GB origin by capturing the changes in fluorescence color.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing performance of CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs to GB from different origin
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the feasibility and stability of the CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs sensing mechanism in the identification of GB origin, the sensing performance of CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs on GB origin was investigated. CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs were reacted with GB extracts from different sources, and their fluorescence spectral data were collected and their pictures under UV lamp irradiation. The fluorescence visualization results (Fig. S8) exhibited that the original GB from different origins had blue fluorescence, which was difficult to distinguish. However, after the reaction with the composite material, the GB extracted from Ningxia, Xinjiang, Gansu, Inner Mongolia and Qinghai showed five different fluorescence, and the fluorescence emission spectra changed red or blue shifted to a certain extent. The difference was thereby amplified. The experimental results demonstrated that the sensing mechanism of CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs had some feasibility in GB origin identification, which laid the foundation for the subsequent rapid visual identification of GB from different origins.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GB origin identification tracing based on fluorescence spectroscopic and visual RGB data
Precise traceability of GB based on fluorescence spectroscopic data
ce:section.ce:para: From the fluorescence spectroscopic results ( Fig. 5), the original fluorescence spectra of GB in different origins had strong fluorescence emission around 425.0 nm, and the fluorescence spectra of 5 groups of parallel samples were stacked and difficult to distinguish. However, after the reaction with the composite material, the GB extract from Ningxia, Xinjiang, Gansu, Inner Mongolia and Qinghai showed five different fluorescences, and the fluorescence emission spectra changed to red or blue shifted to a certain extent. The difference was thereby amplified. However, Gansu and Ningxia GB mixture produced similar color. The reason might be that Gansu and Ningxia origin adjacent with similar geographical and climatic conditions, which led to similar growth, metabolism and accumulation components of GB.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Accurate GB traceability based on visual RGB data
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the identification of the nano-effect fluorescence spectrum, a portable single-channel visual array sensor was further used to achieve accurate visualization identification of GB from different origins.

To further demonstrate the discriminative effect of single-channel composite visual sensors CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs, we established a PLS-DA model based on the colour changes of GB and CdTe-QDs @ N-CDs complexes. Firstly, a 10 × 10 pixel grid was constructed to extract images from each sensor point using Photoshop software. 30 sets of 10 × 10 images were obtained for each origin of GB. The acquired image data was then converted into RGB values using MATLAB R2021b software, yielding fluorescence RGB visualization data. PLS-DA modelling was performed on the obtained fluorescence spectral data and RGB visualization data to identify the geographical origins of GB. The PLS-DA algorithm model was implemented on the MATLAB 7.10.0 (R2010a) platform (Mathworks, Natick, MA. USA) based on the in-house computational code scripts written by the authors. The classification results were given in Table 1 and Fig. S9.

This study explored a new method combining single-channel composite fluorescent sensors and chemometrics to rapidly visual identify the authentic origin of GB. CdTe-QDs and N-CDs were synthesized as complexes fluorescent sensors, amplifying the autofluorescence signal of GB upon reaction with phenolic acid esters. Firstly, phenolic acid compounds found in GB can engage in electron competition on the CdTe-QDs via the PET effect, resulting in reduced CdTe-QDs fluorescence. Secondly, phenolic acids can effectively suppress the fluorescence of N-CDs through the ACQ. In addition, the resulting sensor images were preprocessed using Photoshop and MATLAB R2016a, and feature information was integrated and enhanced. A PLS-DA model utilizing fluorescence spectral data and visual RGB values achieved recognition accuracies of 97.9% and 93.3% for identifying GB production areas. Furthermore, the training and predicted of GB origin identification based on visual RGB data were 100% and 93.3%, which can realize the rapid visual identification of GB from five large producing regions. The notable color response of the fluorescent materials was linked to phenolic acid presence in GB, enabling quality evaluation and scientific support for GB production and market management. This approach offers new perspectives for evaluating other drugs and medications.

Fu Hai-yan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Yao Fangmi: Investigation. Qi Linlin: Validation, Investigation. Long Wanjun: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Suo Yixin: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Marchioni Eric: Supervision. Hu Zikang: Validation. Guan Yuting: Validation. Lei Guanghua: Investigation. Zhou Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Zhao Minjie: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 32122068, 22208381) and Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province (2022CFB458), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, South-Central Minzu University (CZZ23012; CZQ23044). The authors thank the Analytical & Measuring Center, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-Central Minzu University for the spectral analyses.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.135438.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yixin Suo received her Bachelor degree from South-Central Minzu University in 2021 and studied for a master's degree in medicine. Her major is Traditional Chinese pharmacy, mainly focusing on nanosensors combined with stoichiometry in the traceability of traditional Chinese medicine.

Wanjun Long received her Bachelor degree from Hengyang Normal University in 2016 and obtained Ph.D. degree under the supervision of Prof. Hai-Long Wu in the College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Hunan University in 2021. She joined Prof. Haiyan Fu’s research group at School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-Central Minzu University. Her research involves in fluorescence sensors and rapid detection methods for food safety.

Linlin Qi Studying in South-Central University for Nationalities. Her majored in pharmaceutical analysis, mainly focusing on the application of nano effects combined with stoichiometry for food and herb traceability.

Fangmi Yao Studying in South-Central University for Nationalities. Her majored in pharmaceutical analysis, mainly focusing on the application of nanosensors combined with stoichiometry for food and herb traceability.

Guanghua Lei receivd his Bachelor degree from South-Central Minzu University in 2021 and he is a Master student supervised by Prof. Haiyan Fu in South-Central Minzu University. His research focuses on new methods based on nanosensors and nanospectroscopy applied to the quality control of food and traditional Chinese medicine.

Yuting Guan received her Bachelor degree from South-Central Minzu University in 2021 and studied for a master's degree in medicine. Her major is Traditional Chinese pharmacy, mainly focusing on the application research of polyspectral technology combined with stoichiometry in the traceability of traditional Chinese medicine.

Zikang Hu received her Bachelor degree from Wuhan Polytechnic University in 2020. Currently, she is a Master’s student supervised by Prof. Haiyan Fu in South-Central Minzu University. Her research involves the construction of sensing methods based on nanomaterials and rapid detection methods for food safety.

Prof. Eric Marchioni is the and associate professor of University of strasbourg (P. R. France). He received his Ph.D. in analytical chemistry from University of Strasbourg (P. R. China). His research focuses on the construction of new mass-spectrometric technique mass and their applications in food and drug species identification, origin tracing, disease prevention and control.

Prof. Minjie Zhao is the and associate professor of University of strasbourg (P. R. France). She received her Ph.D. in analytical chemistry from University of Strasbourg (P. R. China). Her research focuses on the construction of new mass spectrometry methods and their applications in food species identification, origin tracing, disease prevention and control.

Prof. Li Zhou is the and associate professor of College of Pharmacy at South-Central Minzu University (P. R. China). She received her Ph.D. in analytical chemistry from University of Strasbourg (P. R. China) in 2013. She then worked at Nanjing Agricultural University (P. R. China) until 2018 before moving to Nanjing Agricultural University. Her research focuses on the construction of new lipidomics and metabolomics mass spectrometry methods and their applications in food and drug species identification, origin tracing, disease prevention and control.

Prof. Haiyan Fu is a member of the Academic Committee and the head of the Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis of South-Central Minzu University. She received her Ph.D. in analytical chemistry from Hunan University in 2010. Then she works in the School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-Central Minzu University and became a full professor in 2017. Her research mainly focuses on the development and application of new methods for drug and food security through chemometrics combined with nanometer materials and spectroscopic techniques. Prof. Fu has over 100 publications, including Biosen. Bioelectron., Carbon, Sensor Actuat. B: Chem., Anal. Chem., Food Chem., Anal. Chim. Acta., etc.
